perturbation.original_text: The term “ person ” as used in this letter agreement shall be broadly interpreted to including the media and any corporation, partnership, group, individual or other entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ person ” as used in this letter agreement shall be narrowly interpreted to include only natural persons and not any corporation, partnership, group, or other entity.
perturbation.explanation: The modification restricts the definition of 'person' to exclude corporations and other entities, contradicting established legal precedent and common usage in contract law. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: 1 U.S.C. § 1 states that 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise… the words 'person' and 'whoever' include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.' This contract language directly contradicts this rule of construction by explicitly excluding corporations and other entities from the definition of 'person'.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 1 U.S.C. § 1, which states that 'the words “person” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.' The changed text explicitly excludes corporations and other entities from the definition of 'person', thus creating a legal contradiction, because the new text would exclude an entity that is legally required to be included.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished by Captaris hereunder, Vector hereby agrees that, for a period of twelve months from the date hereof, it will not, and will not encourage or assist its affiliates to, solicit for employment (a) any officers or other senior key employees of Captaris or any of its business units so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris, and (b) any of the other employees of Captaris or any of its business units with whom Vector has had contact or who Vector first became aware of during the period of its investigation of Captaris, so long as they are employed by the Captaris or its business units, without obtaining the prior written consent of Captaris.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished by Captaris hereunder, Vector hereby agrees that it will not solicit for employment any employees of Captaris for a period of twelve months, regardless of employment status or consent.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows solicitation with consent or if the employee initiates discussions. The modification removes these exceptions, creating a blanket prohibition. This potentially creates an unreasonable restraint on trade, contradicting California law regarding non-compete agreements, since the contract is signed in CA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that 'Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The modified text creates a broad restriction on soliciting *any* employee, regardless of whether that employee initiates contact or has left Captaris, which could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint on trade, violating California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the restriction on soliciting employees, removing exceptions present in the original text, which allowed solicitation with consent or if the employee initiated discussions. This creates a blanket prohibition. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as stated in both scraped snippets, renders void "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind" except as provided in that chapter. The modified contract, by broadly prohibiting the solicitation of employees, potentially creates an unlawful restraint on trade, directly conflicting with the statute's intent as it removes the original exceptions.

perturbation.original_text: Vector agrees that, for a period (the “ Standstill Period ”) ending on the earliest of (i) six months from the date of this agreement and (ii) the occurrence of a “Significant Event” (as defined below), unless it shall have been specifically invited in writing by Captaris, neither Vector nor any of its affiliates (as such term is defined under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “ Exchange Act ”)) will in any manner, directly or indirectly, (a) effect or publicly seek, offer or propose to effect, or cause or participate in or in any way assist any other person to effect or publicly seek, offer or propose to effect or participate in, (i) any acquisition of 5% or more of the securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or consolidated assets (including securities of subsidiaries) of Captaris; (ii) any tender or exchange offer, merger or other business combination involving Captaris or any of its subsidiaries; (iii) any recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or other extraordinary transaction with respect to Captaris or any of its subsidiaries; or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents to vote any voting securities of Captaris
perturbation.changed_text: Vector agrees that, for a period (the “ Standstill Period ”) lasting indefinitely, unless it shall have been specifically invited in writing by Captaris, neither Vector nor any of its affiliates will in any manner, directly or indirectly, effect any acquisition of securities of Captaris.
perturbation.explanation: By making the standstill period indefinite, the modification likely violates the principle of reasonableness in contract law and could be deemed an unenforceable restraint on trade. The original six-month period and the defined 'Significant Event' provide reasonable limitations, which are now absent.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Captaris, Inc. is not explicitly stated to be incorporated in Delaware, many public companies are, and the contract deals with potential acquisitions. Delaware General Corporation Law § 203 places limits on business combinations with interested stockholders, generally providing a 3-year restriction unless certain conditions are met. An indefinite standstill period could be viewed as circumventing the protections and limitations outlined in this law, especially if it effectively prevents Vector from engaging in transactions otherwise permitted after a defined period under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by changing the standstill period from a defined period to an indefinite one. The Law Explanation suggests this could circumvent protections and limitations under Delaware law, especially if it prevents Vector from engaging in transactions otherwise permitted after a defined period. While the provided snippet discusses mergers and related corporate actions within Delaware law (§ 203), it implies a time-bound framework for restrictions on stockholders. Making the standstill indefinite could be seen as an attempt to bypass these time limits, creating a direct contradiction because it removes a defined period within which certain actions are restricted.

perturbation.original_text: It is further understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this letter agreement by either party or any of its Representatives and that the parties shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this letter agreement but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: It is further understood and agreed that equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, shall be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of this letter agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By making equitable relief the exclusive remedy, the modification contradicts the principle that monetary damages are often an available and appropriate remedy for breach of contract. This could be deemed unconscionable if equitable relief is inadequate to compensate for the breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719 (though this agreement isn't a sale of goods, the principle applies to contracts generally) addresses contractual modification or limitation of remedy.  It states that resort to a remedy as provided is optional *unless* the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. However, it also states that 'where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act.' The change makes equitable relief exclusive, but if such relief is insufficient, the affected party may still be entitled to other remedies under law (such as money damages), even if the contract intends to prevent that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. perturbation.justification: The modified text makes equitable relief the sole remedy, contradicting the principle that monetary damages are often available for breach of contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically UCC § 2-719, states that a remedy is optional unless expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. However, it continues that 'where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act.' The original contract allowed for both equitable relief and other remedies. The change eliminates the possibility of other remedies, even if equitable relief fails, thus creating a legal contradiction. The change effectively attempts to eliminate the potential for remedies 'as provided in this Act,' which may include monetary damages, even though the UCC language suggests such a limitation may not be fully enforceable if the exclusive remedy fails its essential purpose.

perturbation.original_text: This letter agreement is for the benefit of the parties and their respective directors, officers, stockholders, owners, affiliates, and agents, and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This letter agreement is solely for the benefit of Vector Capital Corporation and Captaris, Inc., and shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies New York law, but the modified text indicates the contract will be under California law. Since the contract is signed in CA, it defaults to CA law unless explicitly stated otherwise. Changing the governing law without a clear justification or connection to California could lead to disputes over interpretation and enforcement, and violates common expectations. This is made more problematic since the original text benefited other parties, now taken away.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 deals with choice of law provisions. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law, the chosen law must have a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or there must be a reasonable basis for the parties' choice. If not, the choice-of-law provision may be disregarded if it would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which would be the state of the applicable rule of law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Switching from NY law to CA law and restricting the agreement's beneficiaries without a clear connection to California violates the spirit of respecting party autonomy in choice of law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal principles regarding choice of law, as described in the Law Explanation. The change from New York law to California law, coupled with the restriction of beneficiaries, raises concerns under Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. While the provided scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation indicates that choice-of-law provisions must have a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction, or a reasonable basis for the choice. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that changing the governing law without justification, and restricting beneficiaries, creates a legal contradiction. The original contract benefited multiple parties, now exclusively benefiting Vector Capital Corporation and Captaris, Inc.
